risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomisation was conducted using pre-generated random permuted blocks (block sizes of four and six). A data manager at the York Trials Unit, who was completely independent of the research team, created the randomisation programme, which was used to allocate participants to one of the two treatment arms" Comment: This describes a proper random allocation process with pre-generated random permuted blocks and an independent central randomisation programme, which is sufficient to judge the sequence generation as truly random.

Quote: "After completing the baseline assessment, the research nurse telephoned a secure and remote randomisation service, located at the York Trials Unit (University of York, UK)." Comment: This describes central allocation via a secure remote randomisation service, which would prevent the recruiting nurse from foreseeing the next assignment before enrolment; therefore allocation concealment is at low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The study explicitly states that participants and staff could not be blinded to treatment allocation. It says, "staff and participants could not be blinded to treatment," and treatment was started immediately after randomisation with NPWT versus standard care delivered by treating nurses. Comment: Because this was an unblinded comparison of interventions where knowledge of allocation could plausibly affect wound care, dressing changes, co-interventions, and patient-reported outcomes such as pain and quality of life, the dominant risk for this domain is high.

Quote: "A blinded outcome assessor (experienced Tissue Viability nurse not involved in the day-to-day conduct of the trial) then assessed photographs for (1) assessment of healing, (2) threats to blinding and (3) picture quality." and "The research nurse was primarily responsible for data collection"; also "Participants were asked to complete the EQ-5D HR-QoL questionnaire" and ulcer-related pain was measured on a "10 cm visual analogue scale". Comment: The healing outcome was assessed by an independent blinded assessor from photographs, but the study also collected patient-reported pain and EQ-5D quality-of-life data, which are subjective and would be influenced if participants were not blinded (the patients are the assessors for these outcomes). Under the worst-outcome rule, the presence of unblinded subjective outcomes makes the overall detection-bias domain high risk even though blinded assessment was used for wound photographs.

Quote: "There was no attrition recorded during this trial. From the 12 recruited participants, 5 (2 NPWT and 3 SC participants) exited the trial after completing the maximum 6-month follow-up. A further five participants (two NPWT and three SC) completed less than the 6-month follow-up as they were recruited later into the study. Two participants died during the trial" Comment: The paper reports no loss to follow-up/attrition at the analysed time point and provides a CONSORT-style accounting of all 12 participants. Although follow-up duration varied because of staggered recruitment and 2 deaths occurred, there is no evidence of missing outcome data or differential loss between arms that would bias the results.
